summary: I'm originally from Uganda where I completed my medical school. Coming from a family with many doctors inspired me to pursue medicine. Uganda is where my journey in healthcare began before I moved abroad for further research and training.

Around 1991, I came to do research focused on HIV because I was really interested in that field. However, the research opportunities were less than I expected, so I shifted my plans towards clinical medicine, doing residency and fellowship instead. HIV research was the initial reason for my move, but it led me to a broader medical career.

I did my residency in New York City, which was an important step in my medical training. Living there exposed me to a diverse patient population and helped shape my clinical skills. It was one of the first places I lived in the U.S. after leaving Uganda.

After completing my fellowship, I took a job in Indiana where I worked for five years. It was a significant period in my career as I gained practical experience in family medicine. Despite the time I spent there, I eventually decided to return to Minnesota because of the welcoming community.

Before moving to Indiana, my family and I lived in Virginia for some time. It was part of our journey through different states as I pursued my medical training and career. Virginia was one of the places where we settled briefly during this transition period.

I completed my fellowship in geriatrics at the University of Minnesota. This fellowship helped me specialize further in family medicine, focusing on comprehensive care for all ages. The university was a pivotal place in my training and later influenced my decision to settle in Minnesota.

Minnesota became home for me and my family after my fellowship and work experiences elsewhere. I found the people here very welcoming and warm, which made me want to stay, especially since my children were born here and this is their culture now. The sense of community in Minnesota is strong, and I appreciate how people support each other, which aligns with my view of life as a complete circle.
